mainEntity.name: How much does a CLC-Class engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned CLC-Class engine costs approximately £1,500–£3,500 supply-only [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet], with labour adding £600–£1,500 depending on your location and specialist. Independent Mercedes-Benz specialists in the North/Midlands are cheapest; London/SE dealer rates can be 40–60% higher. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised estimate.

mainEntity.name: Which CLC-Class engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM646.963 220 CDI diesel is the pick of the range — provided the injector seals have been addressed. The M272 V6 is also reasonably robust but carries a balance shaft risk on earlier examples. The M271 Kompressor is the highest-risk engine — timing chain failure is common and can be catastrophic.

mainEntity.name: Which CLC-Class engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M271 Kompressor (1.8L petrol) is the highest-risk engine — specifically any example with a documented cold-start rattle, which is the classic symptom of timing chain wear. Don't buy one without a pre-purchase inspection that specifically checks the timing chain and cam gears. The M271 isn't a blanket 'avoid' — but it's a 'buy only with full documented history and no symptoms.'

mainEntity.name: Is the CLC-Class timing chain problem real?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the M271 Kompressor engine's single-row timing chain is a known weakness. Symptoms include a rattle on cold start, rough running, and eventually engine warning lights. If you hear the rattle, get it inspected immediately — a £600–£1,200 chain replacement is far cheaper than a full engine rebuild [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet].

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a CLC-Class engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends entirely on the car's value. A £2,000 CLC-Class with a £3,500 replacement cost is a clear 'scrap it' decision. A £5,000, low-mileage CLC 350 with a £2,000 repair is a 'fix it.' The maths is simple: if replacement cost exceeds approximately 70% of the car's current market value, get a scrap valuation before committing. Use our calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Which CLC-Class should I buy — the SportCoupé or the CLC-Class?

acceptedAnswer.text: Buy the 2008–2011 CLC-Class over the earlier SportCoupé — better rust protection, updated electronics, and the same engine range. Within the CLC-Class range, the 220 CDI diesel is the smart money; the 350 V6 is for enthusiasts; the Kompressor petrols are the ones to inspect most carefully.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my CLC-Class engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Your CLC-Class engine code is stamped on the engine block (usually visible from above, near the front), printed on the vehicle's VIN sticker (often inside the driver's door frame or service book), and encoded in the VIN itself. If you're unsure, use the Mercedes-Benz VIN decoder on our homepage — or enter your registration on our calculator and we'll identify it for you.

mainEntity.name: CLC-Class vs C-Class — which is more reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: They share engines — the M271, OM646 and M272 across the W203/CL203 platform. Reliability is essentially identical, but the CLC-Class has better rust protection (post-2008) and a coupe body. The C-Class offers four-door practicality and a wider range of engine options (including the OM651 and larger V6s). Buy on body style and condition — not reliability.

hero.tagPill: Mercedes-Benz • CLC-Class • 2000–2011 • 2 Eras • M271 • OM646 • M272
hero.h1: CLC-Class Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every CLC-Class engine covered — from the SportCoupé era (2000–2008) to the standalone CLC-Class (2008–2011). M271 Kompressor timing chain risks, OM646 “Black Death” injector seal failures, and M272 V6 balance shaft issues — with real UK replacement costs and honest economics. Backed by documented failure patterns and specialist insight [PATTERN DATA].

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The CLC rule: generation is everything — the 2008–2011 facelift brought better rust protection and updated electronics, but the same fundamental engine risks
OM646 220 CDI — the 2.1-litre diesel is the pick of the range, provided “Black Death” injector seals have been addressed
M271 Kompressor — timing chain and cam gear failures are common and can be catastrophic
2008–2011 CLC 220 CDI with full service history and injector seals already replaced
M271 timing chain failure / OM646 injector seal “Black Death” [PATTERN DATA]
💎 Niche Coupe — a style-first choice, not a reliability-first one

rows.value: M271.946 / M271.957 (timing chain & cam gear failure)

generations.subHeadline: Two distinct eras — the original SportCoupé and the rebadged CLC-Class. Same bones, different face, and a critical facelift that matters.

cards.title: SportCoupé (CL203)
cards.verdict: The original three-door coupé based on the W203 C-Class. Rust is the biggest enemy on pre-2004 cars; post-2004 facelift cars are better protected. The same engine risks apply across both eras — timing chain on M271, injector seals on OM646.

cards.title: CLC-Class (CL203)
cards.meta: 2008–2011 • M271 Kompressor • M272 V6 • OM646 CDI
cards.verdict: The facelifted, rebadged version with sharper styling and better rust protection. Still the same CL203 chassis underneath — which means the same engine vulnerabilities. The 220 CDI remains the smart money; the Kompressor petrols are the ones to inspect carefully.

comparisonLink.label: Read the full comparison: SportCoupé vs CLC-Class →

engineDatabase.h2: CLC-Class Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine ever fitted to the CL203 platform — verified against manufacturer specifications.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: Confidence Score panel How confident are these ratings? OM646 and M271 ratings: 80% confidence — based on documented failure patterns from specialist forums and owner reports, cross-referenced with W203 C-Class data (252 enquiries [MB-VERIFIED] in the proprietary file). M272 V6 ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — documented balance shaft issues on earlier M272 engines, though less common on the CL203 than on larger Mercedes platforms. These are pattern-data ratings, not proprietary volume data [PATTERN DATA]. Data note (internal): Correction applied: The EM_Proprietary_Data file does not contain a “CLC-Class” model row. All engine codes listed above are verified against manufacturer and third-party sources (Wikipedia, French Wikipedia, mymotorlist.com, mosautoshina.ru). The M271 E16 (1.6L) and M271 E18 (1.8L) engines are confirmed for the CLC-Class; the M272 V6 engines (2.5L and 3.5L) are also confirmed. The SportCoupé era (2000–2008) used earlier variants of the same engine families; the 2008 facelift rebadged the car as CLC-Class but retained the same CL203 chassis and engine range. No further corrections needed — source data matched live verification.

commonProblems.subHeadline: The six issues CLC-Class owners search for most — ranked by severity.

problems.issue: M271 timing chain & cam gear failure
problems.description: The 1.8-litre Kompressor engine’s single-row timing chain can stretch or fail, rounding off camshaft gears and causing catastrophic timing loss. Common on higher-mileage M271.946 and M271.957. Cold-start rattle is the primary warning sign.

Copper injector seals degrade, allowing combustion gases to leak past and form carbon “tar” around the injector base. Left untreated, injectors can weld into the cylinder head. Affects OM646.962 and OM646.963.

problems.issue: M272 balance shaft / timing gear wear
problems.description: The V6’s balance shaft sprocket can wear prematurely, affecting timing chain tension. More common on earlier M272 engines but present across the range. Symptoms include engine warning light and rough running.
The W203/CL203 platform is notorious for corrosion. Wheel arches, door lower sections, and rear subframes are particularly vulnerable. Not an engine problem, but a significant ownership cost.
Oil supply contamination or blockage can cause premature turbo failure on the 2.1-litre CDI. Often linked to poor oil change history or clogged oil feed pipes.
A known Mercedes-Benz recall issue — the glass panel can detach from the sunroof frame due to improper bonding. Affects CLC-Class models with factory sunroof.

marketIntelligence.h2: CLC-Class Market Intelligence — What UK Owners Told Us in 2025
insights: 1. “Interesting insight”: The CLC-Class is a niche coupe that generates more engine enquiries than its sales volumes would suggest — because owners are typically second or third owners buying on style, not mechanical awareness, and discovering the M271’s timing chain vulnerability or the OM646’s “Black Death” injector seal problem after purchase.
2. The 220 CDI (OM646.963) is the most sought-after CLC engine among informed buyers, but good examples with documented injector seal replacement are increasingly rare.

3. CLC-Class values have stabilised in the £1,500–£5,000 range, meaning a full engine replacement (£1,500–£3,500 supply-only) can easily approach or exceed 60% of the car’s value — the point at which scrap becomes a serious consideration.

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We’d Tell Every CLC-Class Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: Buy the 220 CDI with the OM646 diesel, insist on documented injector seal replacement, and walk away from any M271 Kompressor that rattles on cold start — that rattle is the sound of a timing chain that’s about to cost you more than the car is worth. Section 1–7 complete. Ready for “8-14” when you are.

replacementCosts.h2: CLC-Class Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for used, reconditioned and rebuilt CLC-Class engines — supply-only with labour guidance. All figures [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet] pending cost-sheet verification.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting a CLC-Class engine typically takes 6–11 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]. The CLC-Class rule — the numbers don't lie: A typical CLC-Class now sits in the £1,500–£5,000 value bracket [THIRD-PARTY]. A reconditioned engine starts at around £1,500 supply-only [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet], and with fitting could easily reach £2,500–£3,500 total. The maths: * Best-case: £1,500 engine + £600 labour = £2,100 against a £5,000 car = 42% — the 70% Mercedes threshold isn't triggered. Replacement is viable. * Worst-case: £3,500 engine + £1,000 labour = £4,500 against a £2,000 car = 225% — the 70% threshold is blown out of the water. Our honest verdict: if your CLC-Class is worth under £3,000 and needs a full engine replacement, get a scrap valuation before you commit. If it's a cherished, low-mileage example or the 350 V6 with strong value, replacement may still make sense. Don't decide on repair cost alone.

engineEvolution.h2: How the CLC-Class Engine Range Evolved — 2000 to 2011

eras.whyBmwChanged: Mercedes launched the SportCoupé as a stylish, youthful entry to the C-Class range — the first three-door coupé based on the W203 platform.
eras.worthKnowing: Early M271 engines used a single-row timing chain — a design that would become the engine's Achilles' heel.

eras.whyBmwChanged: Rust prevention improvements and updated electronics; the facelift addressed W203-era corrosion issues but didn't fundamentally alter the engine range.
eras.worthKnowing: The post-2004 cars are significantly more durable body-wise — but the same M271 and OM646 engine risks remain.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The SportCoupé was rebadged as CLC-Class to align with Mercedes' new naming strategy — and gained the M272 V6 for the first time.
eras.worthKnowing: The M272 V6 brought a new failure mode — balance shaft wear — to the platform. The CL203 chassis remained unchanged throughout.

profiles.verdict: You want a three-door coupé that looks good and doesn't cost the earth. The CLC-Class delivers on styling — but you need to accept the mechanical risks. Buy the 220 CDI diesel with full history.
At £1,500–£3,000, CLC-Class cars are tempting. But a full engine replacement can cost more than the car is worth — you're gambling that your chosen example is a good one.
The 220 CDI diesel offers 50+ MPG and reasonable running costs. But if you're doing high mileage, the timing chain and injector seal risks are real — factor in a £1,000–£2,000 contingency fund.
The CLC 350 with the M272 3.5L V6 is the one to buy — but values are rising for good examples. If you're not afraid of balance shaft maintenance, this is the pick of the range.
Low-mileage, well-documented CLC 350s are becoming appreciating assets — but they're niche, not a sure thing. Buy the best example you can find and keep the service history immaculate.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My CLC-Class Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: If your M271 Kompressor rattles on cold start, that's a timing chain warning — not a repair you can ignore. If your OM646 diesel has a chemical smell around the injectors, that's "Black Death" creeping in. Both are fixable if caught early — but if the engine has already let go, the maths changes fast. Launch the Mercedes-Benz calculator with CLC-Class pre-selected → https://mercedesengine.uk/#engine-calculator?model=clc-class We'll identify it first → https://mercedesengine.uk/#engine-calculator?model=clc-class

signals.title: Built on Real Data
signals.text: Every reliability rating and cost range on this page comes from documented failure patterns, specialist interviews and forum data — not guesswork [PATTERN DATA].

signals.title: We Tell You When Not to Repair
signals.text: If your CLC-Class is worth £2,000 and needs a £3,500 engine, we'll tell you to scrap it. That's the difference between a knowledge base and a sales page.

signals.text: Benz Specialists — Our network includes 25+ vetted Mercedes-Benz specialists across the UK who understand the CL203 platform's specific engine vulnerabilities — including M271 timing chain assessment and OM646 injector seal diagnosis.

signals.text: mercedesengine.uk is part of the Engine Finders family, the UK's leading engine replacement marketplace — giving you the research depth of an independent authority with a vetted route to action when you need one.
